critique.summary: A study led by Central Queensland University and the University of Bristol found that gamblers who receive regular direct marketing offers place more bets, spend more money, and experience greater gambling-related harms compared to those who opt out. The research provides evidence of a causal link between exposure to gambling marketing and increased gambling harm, supporting calls for tighter regulations.
mainPoints: Gamblers receiving direct marketing offers placed 23% more bets and spent 39% more money than those who opted out.
Participants exposed to marketing reported 67% more short-term gambling harms such as distress.
The study was a randomized controlled trial involving 227 regular sports and race bettors in Australia.
The findings challenge the UK Government's 2023 position that there was no causal link between marketing and gambling harm.
Personal testimony from a recovered gambler highlights the manipulative nature of personalized gambling marketing.
Researchers and advocacy groups call for tighter restrictions or bans on direct gambling marketing to protect consumers.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are supported by a peer-reviewed study published in the journal Addiction, which is a reputable source. The randomized controlled trial design strengthens the causal inference between direct marketing exposure and increased gambling harm. The involvement of recognized academic institutions and funding from Gambling Research Australia further supports the credibility. The article also references a real individual’s experience consistent with known patterns of gambling addiction and marketing influence. Overall, the article accurately represents the study's findings and their implications.
considerations: The study was conducted primarily with Australian participants, which may limit generalizability to other countries with different gambling regulations and cultures.
The article focuses on direct marketing but acknowledges that other forms of gambling advertising (e.g., TV, social media) may have similar effects, which warrants further research.
Long-term effects beyond the two-week monitoring period were not discussed and could provide additional insights into sustained harm.
Potential confounding factors such as individual susceptibility to gambling addiction or socioeconomic status were not detailed in the summary.
The article does not discuss possible counterarguments or perspectives from the gambling industry or regulatory bodies.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the harms of gambling marketing and the ethical and regulatory challenges surrounding the gambling industry. Commenters highlight the parallels with tobacco and alcohol advertising, criticize government inaction, and debate the effectiveness and ethics of gambling promotions.

comments.author: beloch
comments.text:
>“Although the findings relate to direct marketing, I see no reason why the same or similar adverse effects wouldn’t occur for gambling advertising on TV or social media.”

Controlling/banning advertising for Alcohol and Tobacco results in significant health benefits. Sports gambling used to be illegal in many places or limited to specific places. Now that it's available in your pocket, like a pack of smokes or a flask of whisky, why wouldn't advertising triggers, direct or otherwise, be effective at encouraging susceptible people to partake? This is not a surprising result. It's the inaction of most governments that is surprising.
comments.analysis: This comment draws a well-supported analogy between gambling and other regulated harmful products, emphasizing the impact of advertising and government inaction.

comments.author: apt-apt-apt-apt
comments.text: Study seems flawed– it preselects two different audiences (allow/not-allow offers mapping to something like low/high interest in gambling), then attributes the offers as the cause rather than the effect of selecting different audiences.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid methodological critique about causality and sample selection bias, which is important for interpreting the study's conclusions.

comments.author: tim-projects
comments.text:
Fun fact: gambling companies increasingly also invest in mental health service companies, so that they can profit from both sides.

That's what they mean when they say the house always wins.
comments.analysis: Highlights a cynical but plausible conflict of interest in the gambling industry profiting from both gambling and mental health treatment.

comments.author: abcde666777
comments.text:
I've always found the marketing around gambling (and most things really) completely disgusting. As a society I think we're far too tolerant of these things.

A lot of the ads basically go along the lines of: 'you could win big and have a great time, awesome! (disclaimer: will probably ruin your life)'.

It should be like it is with smoking - photos of lung cancer patients on the package. People will still do it of course but at least it's not falsely advertised.

So the gambling ads should be things like, that moment where your wife finds out you've drained the family's savings and the house is about to be re-possessed. Yeah.
comments.analysis: Expresses a strong emotional response and advocates for more honest advertising, drawing parallels to tobacco warning labels.

comments.author: ludston
comments.text:
I mean if it didn't make the gambling organizations more money they wouldn't do it. Gambling industry has always been about how much wealth it can extract from the punters without being regulated for it.

Hopefully this research ends up being used to justify more gambling regulations, but governments are addicted to the gambling lobby donations so who knows what will happen.
comments.analysis: Points out the financial incentives behind gambling marketing and regulatory capture, a well-documented issue in policy.

comments.author: Invictus0
comments.text: why can't we have a law that just caps your gambling losses? Everyone gets a federally issued gambling license tied to your ID, if you lose more than X amount the casino is no longer legally allowed to let you play. Casual gamblers can still enjoy, problem gamblers get cut off; just like with alcohol at the bar.
comments.analysis: Proposes a regulatory solution to limit gambling harm, showing constructive engagement with the problem.

comments.author: xenadu02
comments.text:
Super shocking (sarcasm).

Gamblers are the whales of that industry. The industry is well aware of that and well aware of how much harm they can cause. But their paychecks depend on not knowing so they choose not to.

Same as pay-to-win freemium games. Find the whales and milk them for all you can. For every high-spender who can afford it they know full well the other 99 cannot. They know they are ruining some people's lives. They know they use dirty psychological manipulation tactics. Their paychecks depend on not knowing so they choose not to.
comments.analysis: Accurately describes the exploitative nature of gambling companies targeting high-spending users despite known harms.

comments.author: utopiah
comments.text:
Can't wait to see how this (rather unsurprising yet important finding) is going to get abused for and with AI :

"Hey, I see we haven't chat / you didn't vibe code for few days now, how about you get 1000 free tokens and we just see where that lead us?"

It perfectly aligns with sycophantic interaction and then roulette outcome one gets, sure it might not work 100% of the time but it works most of the time and "I" as a user somehow "get it" more than AI researcher so "I" can get it to work for me.

Brilliant.
comments.analysis: Offers a speculative but plausible concern about AI-driven marketing exacerbating gambling harm.

comments.author: deaux
comments.text:
I hate this kind of research. Or well, I hate that it has any influence at all, that it matters.

This kind of research being that which shows an obvious harm that we all know about. It should have zero influence because it is blindingly obvious. Namely because if the title wasn't true, betting companies wouldn't be spending lots of money on it in the first place. But they are, as everyone who lives in the UK can tell. So we know. So this study shouldn't influence policy in the slightest. But it does.

I hate it because there's by definition a gap of years between A. all of us knowing that a phenomenon is harmful B. the study coming out. And then another gap of many years between the study and actual policy changes.

Here's my request to people in academia who do studies like these - which is admittedly a tiny percentage of academia. Just fudge the numbers and publish it a year earlier. Use LLMs to generate the text. It would be a huge boon to society. We all know it's true, so you're not doing anything wrong. Your quest for honesty is hurting everyone. The actual data is pointless.

It's like gathering real data on whether pigeons will indeed eat sunflower seeds if thrown on the ground in front of them, versus just making it up. Maybe such a study hasn't been done yet, but it literally doesn't matter because we know the outcome. There's zero gain from actually doing the study versus saying that yes, pigeons will generally do so.
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration with research timelines and policy delays but advocates unethical data manipulation, undermining credibility.

comments.author: rimbo789
comments.text: The legalization and expansion of gambling was a massive mistake and should be undone as soon as possible.
comments.analysis: A strongly opinionated statement lacking nuance or supporting argumentation, reflecting personal bias.

comments.author: kelseyfrog
comments.text:
If it's so bad for gamblers, why don't they stop?

If gambling orgs do something that you know causes harm, why isn't the a legal sense of responsibility?
comments.analysis: Raises important questions about personal responsibility and legal accountability but oversimplifies complex addiction issues.

comments.author: mothballed
comments.text:
When online sports gambling started in the US they were offering $500-$1000 of free bets to sign up. Very tempting to sign up, even though I don't gamble anymore than about once a decade, but I decided whoever did that offer was probably smarter than me about who would win out in the end.

I've been around the block long enough to know you never take an 'easy profit' deal from someone who is in the business of making money from them while in their own domain.
comments.analysis: Shares personal experience illustrating the attractiveness and risk of gambling offers, supporting the article's premise.

comments.author: recursivedoubts
comments.text:
Crazy how we (the US) just decided as a society that gambling was not only not illegal anymore but that it was perfectly reasonable to integrate it deeply into every sporting event possible in a span of about five years.

We didn't decide that, btw.
comments.analysis: Points out a misconception about societal decision-making but provides limited additional insight.
